programs.name: Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI) & Medicaid Buy-In for Children (MBIC)
programs.tagline: Estimated 2026 savings: $2,434–$5,000+/year. Based on the 2026 Medicare Part B premium of $202.90/month plus potential cost-sharing savings for QMB; MBIC comprehensive coverage value varies by child's needs.

localEntities.name: Local HHSC offices statewide; call 2-1-1 for nearest

programs.intro:
If you have a disabled child whose family earns too much for regular Medicaid, or an elderly loved one struggling with Medicare premiums and co-pays, these Texas programs can provide critical health coverage. MBIC (Medicaid Buy-In for Children) offers full Medicaid benefits for disabled children in families earning up to $8,250 monthly for a family of four. You'll pay a sliding-scale premium based on your income and existing insurance, but your child gets comprehensive coverage including doctor visits, hospital care, therapies, and prescriptions.

For elderly loved ones on Medicare, three related programs help with costs. QMB (Qualified Medicare Beneficiary) pays all Medicare premiums and cost-sharing for individuals with monthly income under $1,350. SLMB (Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiary) covers Medicare Part B premiums ($202.90/month in 2026) for those earning up to $1,616 monthly. QI (Qualifying Individual) also covers Part B premiums for those earning up to $1,816 but operates on a first-come, first-served basis.

All programs require Texas residency and have specific asset limits of $9,950 for individuals or $14,910 for couples (Medicare Savings Programs only — MBIC has no asset test), though your home and one car are typically excluded. Because these programs have strict disability requirements and precise income thresholds, gather documentation carefully before applying at YourTexasBenefits.com.
programs.savingsSource: Medicare Part B premium ($202.90/month) plus potential cost-sharing savings for QMB; MBIC comprehensive coverage value varies by child's needs

summary: Disabled children 0-18 with family income up to 300% FPL
Medicare Savings income limits (individual): QMB under $1,350, SLMB up to $1,616, QI up to $1,816/month
Assets under $9,950 individual/$14,910 couple (Medicare Savings Programs; MBIC has no asset test)
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: MBIC requires disability meeting SSI criteria (child's condition significantly restricts daily activities and is expected to last 12+ months or result in death)
otherRequirements: Texas residency
U.S. citizenship or legal immigrant status
Medicare enrollment (for QMB/SLMB/QI programs)
Unmarried status (for MBIC children)
structuredEligibility.povertyLevelReference: 300% FPL for MBIC, 100-135% FPL for Medicare programs

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at YourTexasBenefits.com or call 2-1-1 for help; processing takes 45-90 days depending on disability determination needs.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect disability determination letters, income proof, Medicare cards, bank statements, and residency documents before starting your application.

steps.title: Choose application method
steps.description: Apply for all programs (MBIC, QMB, SLMB, QI) at YourTexasBenefits.com or via the Your Texas Benefits mobile app. For Medicare Savings Programs specifically, use Form H1200.

steps.title: Submit complete application
steps.description: Submit online, mail to HHSC, or get help by calling 2-1-1. Choose Option 2 and provide the head of household's Social Security number.

steps.title: Confirm receipt
steps.description: Call 2-1-1 within one week to verify your application was received. Processing can take 45-90 days, especially if disability determination is needed.

steps.title: Respond to requests promptly
steps.description: HHSC may request additional documentation. Respond within 10 days to avoid delays. Check your mail and YourTexasBenefits.com account regularly.

applicationGuide.processingTime: 45-90 days, longer if disability determination needed for MBIC
applicationGuide.waitlist: QI program may have waitlists due to limited annual funding
applicationGuide.tip: For MBIC, have your child's SSI determination letter ready, this speeds up the disability verification process significantly.

documentsNeeded: Social Security cards for all household members
Child's disability determination letter or SSI award letter (for MBIC)
Medicare card showing Parts A and B (for QMB/SLMB/QI)
Proof of Texas residency (utility bill, lease agreement, or state-issued document)
Citizenship or immigration documents for all household members
Pay stubs from last 30 days or most recent tax return
Social Security award letters for all recipients
Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months)
Investment account statements (stocks, bonds, annuities)
Life insurance policies showing cash values
Vehicle titles and registration documents
Property deeds or mortgage statements
Pre-need burial contracts or burial insurance policies
Documentation of employer-provided health insurance (for MBIC premium calculation)
Any legal guardianship or power of attorney documents

contacts.label: Texas 2-1-1
contacts.description: Free 24/7 helpline for application help and benefits information

contacts.label: HHSC Benefits Hotline
contacts.description: Medicaid and health benefits questions

contacts.label: Medicare Rights Center
contacts.description: Free counseling on Medicare cost-sharing programs

applicationNotes: MBIC applications require proof of SSI-level disability, having a diagnosis alone isn't sufficient, the child must meet functional limitation criteria
QI program funding is limited and allocated first-come, first-served each year, apply early in the calendar year for best chances
If your elderly loved one has QMB coverage, healthcare providers cannot bill them for Medicare co-pays or deductibles, keep your approval letter handy
Premium amounts for MBIC vary based on family income and employer insurance availability, HHSC calculates this during approval process
relatedPrograms: Medicaid for the Elderly and People with Disabilities (MEPD)

contentSections.text: Asset limits above apply to Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI) only. MBIC for children does not have an asset test — only family income is tested.

tiers.name: QMB (Qualified Medicare Beneficiary)
tiers.benefits: Pays Medicare Part A and B premiums, deductibles, co-insurance, and co-payments

tiers.name: SLMB (Specified Low-Income Medicare Beneficiary)
tiers.benefits: Pays Medicare Part B premium only ($202.90/month in 2026)

tiers.name: QI (Qualifying Individual)
tiers.benefits: Pays Medicare Part B premium only ($202.90/month in 2026)

faqs.question: Can my disabled child get MBIC if our family income is $99,000 a year?
faqs.answer: Yes. For 2026, the income limit for a family of four is $8,250 monthly, which is $99,000 annually. As long as your family's adjusted gross income is below 300% of the federal poverty level, your child likely qualifies.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one's income is just over the QMB limit but they can't afford Medicare costs?
faqs.answer: Check if they qualify for SLMB (income up to $1,616/month) or QI (up to $1,816/month). They may also qualify for Extra Help with prescriptions, which has an even higher individual limit of approximately $2,015/month.

faqs.question: Does my loved one's home count toward the $9,950 asset limit?
faqs.answer: No. Your loved one's primary residence is exempt. For Medicare Savings Programs, countable assets must be under $9,950 (up from $9,660). Note: For children applying for MBIC, there is no asset test at all — only family income is considered.

faqs.question: How long does the MBIC disability determination take?
faqs.answer: If your child already receives SSI, approval is faster since disability is established. Without existing SSI, Texas must verify the disability meets SSI criteria, which can extend processing to 90 days or longer. Having comprehensive medical records ready helps speed this process.

faqs.question: Can I apply for multiple Medicare programs at the same time?
faqs.answer: You only need to submit one application. Texas HHSC will automatically evaluate you for the highest benefit (QMB) and, if you don't qualify, they will automatically consider you for SLMB or QI.

faqs.question: What if my elderly loved one lives in assisted living - can they still get these Medicare programs?
faqs.answer: Yes, assisted living residents can qualify for QMB, SLMB, or QI since these are not considered institutional care. However, if your loved one moves to a nursing home and gets Medicaid coverage there, they may lose these Medicare programs since Medicaid becomes primary.

faqs.question: Will getting MBIC affect my family's taxes or other benefits?
faqs.answer: MBIC coverage itself doesn't count as taxable income. However, the program uses your adjusted gross income for eligibility, so significant income changes could affect renewal. Other means-tested benefits may consider MBIC coverage when determining eligibility, but this varies by program.

faqs.question: What happens if we move to another state while my child has MBIC?
faqs.answer: MBIC coverage ends when you leave Texas since it's a state-specific program. Contact your new state's Medicaid office immediately to apply for similar coverage. Some states have different programs for disabled children, and you'll want continuous coverage during the transition.

programs.name: STAR+PLUS Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) Waiver
programs.tagline: Comprehensive Medicaid coverage plus home care services for seniors at nursing home level of need

localEntities.name: MCOs: Community First Health Plans, Molina Healthcare, Superior HealthPlan, Wellpoint/Anthem; local HHS offices via 211

programs.intro:
For the 2026 fiscal year, the STAR+PLUS Home and Community-Based Services (HCBS) Waiver helps your loved one stay at home instead of moving to a nursing facility. This integrated program coordinates all medical care — including doctors, hospitals, and prescriptions — while providing "wraparound" supports like personal attendants, home modifications (ramps/grab bars), and respite care to give family caregivers a break. To qualify, your loved one must meet the "nursing facility level of care" standard and have a monthly income of $2,982 or less, with assets under $2,000 (their primary home and one vehicle are usually exempt).

While basic medical Medicaid can start quickly, specialized home care services have a multi-year statewide Interest List. Because spots are assigned based on when you signed up, it is vital to call the Texas Long-Term Care Interest List at 1-877-438-5658 to register your loved one today. You do not need to be financially or medically ready just to get on the list, and you can decline services later if the timing isn't right. If your loved one is already in a Medicaid-funded nursing home, ask about "Money Follows the Person" rules, which could help them move home much faster.

summary: Needs nursing home level of care

exemptAssets: Life insurance up to $1,500 face value

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must need nursing facility level of care, daily assistance with bathing, dressing, medication management, or other activities of daily living. Requires physician-signed assessment and state approval.
otherRequirements: Texas resident
Medicaid eligible
Cannot be enrolled in another Medicaid waiver
Must join interest list first due to waitlist

applicationGuide.summary: Join the interest list first by calling 2-1-1, then apply for Medicaid when invited, several years wait for home services.

steps.title: Join the interest list
steps.description: Call the Texas Health and Human Services (HHS) Long-Term Care Interest List directly at 1-877-438-5658. While 2-1-1 is a general entry point, calling the direct line is the fastest way to get on the STAR+PLUS HCBS waitlist. This is a mandatory first step for anyone waiting at home.

steps.title: Wait for invitation
steps.description: Texas will contact you when services become available in your area. Wait times are typically several years, varying by region and priority level.

steps.title: Apply for Medicaid
steps.description: When invited, apply online at yourtexasbenefits.com, call 2-1-1, visit your local HHS office, or mail your application.

steps.title: Complete assessment
steps.description: Your chosen Managed Care Organization (MCO) will conduct the nursing facility level of care assessment to determine your service needs.

steps.title: Select MCO
steps.description: Choose from available plans in your area: Community First, Molina, Superior, or Wellpoint. Benefits and extras vary by plan.

applicationGuide.processingTime: 45 to 90 days once your loved one reaches the top of the Interest List. During this time, the state verifies financial eligibility while your chosen health plan (MCO) completes a required in-home medical assessment.
applicationGuide.waitlist: Statewide wait of several years for home-based services. While the list is strictly chronological, your loved one may bypass this wait through the "Money Follows the Person" policy if they currently reside in a Medicaid-funded nursing home.
applicationGuide.tip: Apply for basic Medicaid immediately if eligible, you'll get medical coverage while waiting for home services.

documentsNeeded: Valid government-issued photo ID
Social Security card
Proof of Texas residency (utility bill, lease, or bank statement)
Social Security award letter or other income proof
Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months)
Property deeds or tax statements
Vehicle titles and registration
Life insurance policies with face values
Pre-need burial contracts or irrevocable burial trusts
Medical records documenting need for daily assistance
Physician certification for nursing facility level of care
Medicare card (both parts) if applicable
Investment documents (stocks, bonds, annuities)
Disability determination letters if under 65

contacts.label: Texas 2-1-1
contacts.description: Interest list enrollment and general assistance

contacts.label: HHSC Benefits Line
contacts.description: Use this number for specific questions regarding an active Medicaid application, to check your current eligibility status, or to update your address and income information. Alternate: 2-1-1, option 2.

applicationNotes: Each spouse applies individually with the same $2,982 income limit, no household scaling
You can remain on other program interest lists while enrolled in STAR+PLUS
MCO selection matters, extras like Weight Watchers subscriptions vary by plan and county
Nursing home residents can transition to community services through STAR+PLUS

contentSections.title: MCO Plan Differences
contentSections.content: Benefits vary by Managed Care Organization. Community First serves central Texas, while Superior operates in Dallas, Hidalgo, and Travis counties. Some plans offer extras like Weight Watchers subscriptions (Dallas/Hidalgo only), camp allowances, or additional asthma supplies. Ask about plan-specific benefits when choosing your MCO.

faqs.question: Can my loved one keep their house if it's worth more than $2,000?
faqs.answer: Yes. The $2,000 asset limit doesn't include your loved one's primary home as long as the equity is $752,000 or less (increased from $730,000 in 2025) and they live there or intend to return. The home is also exempt if a spouse or disabled child lives there.

faqs.question: How long is the waitlist really for home services?
faqs.answer: Statewide wait of several years for home-based services in most areas, though this varies by region and priority level. Crisis cases or those at immediate risk of nursing home placement may get expedited processing. You can apply for basic Medicaid coverage immediately while waiting for home services. The only major way to bypass the wait is if your loved one is already in a Medicaid-funded nursing home and uses the "Money Follows the Person" policy — they can often transition back to the community with STAR+PLUS HCBS waiver services much faster than those waiting at home.

faqs.question: Can I apply for my loved one who has dementia?
faqs.answer: Yes. Family members can apply on behalf of aging loved ones, especially those with cognitive impairment. You may need to become an authorized representative through the application process or provide legal guardianship documentation.

faqs.question: What's the difference between the MCO plans?
faqs.answer: All plans provide the same Medicaid benefits, but "Value-Added Services" vary. For 2026, some plans offer extra vision allowances, dental rewards, or fitness programs. Service areas have also shifted; for example, Wellpoint and UnitedHealthcare now cover large clusters including Dallas, Harris, and Bexar counties, while smaller plans may only serve specific Rural Service Areas.

faqs.question: Can my loved one have STAR+PLUS and Medicare at the same time?
faqs.answer: Yes. STAR+PLUS works alongside Medicare coverage. The program coordinates with Medicare to cover gaps and provide additional services Medicare doesn't cover, like personal attendant services and minor home modifications.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one's income increases after enrollment?
faqs.answer: Report any changes immediately. For 2026, the gross monthly income limit has increased to $2,982. If their income goes above this, they may need a Qualified Income Trust (QIT) to maintain eligibility. Do not assume they are disqualified until you've explored this legal option.

faqs.question: Can couples both be on STAR+PLUS?
faqs.answer: Yes. If both apply, each has an individual income limit of $2,982, but their combined countable asset limit is $3,000 (not $2,000 each). If only one spouse applies, the spouse staying at home can often keep significantly more assets (up to $162,660) to prevent spousal impoverishment. Texas follows the "name on the check" rule, so a spouse's high income won't automatically disqualify an applicant.

faqs.question: What if my loved one needs nursing home care while on the waitlist?
faqs.answer: STAR+PLUS covers nursing facility care immediately upon Medicaid approval, you don't need to wait for HCBS services. Your loved one can later transition from nursing home to community services when home care slots become available.

programs.name: Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE)
programs.tagline: All medical care, adult day center, and home support coordinated under one team, no copays if your loved one has Medicaid.
programs.intro:
If your loved one is 55+ and needs nursing home-level care but wants to stay in their home, PACE provides everything under one coordinated team: primary care, specialists, prescription drugs, adult day center with transportation, home care services, and respite care. Because all services are managed by a single provider, your loved one won't need to coordinate between separate doctors, pharmacies, and care agencies — one team handles everything. Your loved one must be certified by Texas as needing nursing home-level care (daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or mobility) and live in a PACE service area. Most participants have both Medicare and Medicaid, which covers all services with no copays. Private pay is possible if your loved one doesn't qualify for Medicaid but needs comprehensive care coordination.

PACE is only available in limited Texas areas. Major service hubs currently include Amarillo and the Panhandle, Lubbock, El Paso (Bienvivir), San Antonio and Bexar County, the Dallas/Fort Worth area, and the Houston/Harris County region. If your loved one lives outside these specific service areas, PACE is not available.

summary: State-certified need for nursing home level of care
Able to live safely in community
2026 gross monthly income limit: under $2,982 (most participants)
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be state-certified as needing nursing home level of care (requires assessment showing need for daily help with bathing, dressing, medication management, or mobility)
otherRequirements: Live in the service area of a Texas PACE provider
Able to live safely in the community with PACE support
Cannot be enrolled in Medicare Advantage, Medicare prescription drug plan, or hospice
Spousal protection: If the applicant is married, the healthy spouse can retain a significant portion of the couple's assets (up to $162,660 in 2026).

applicationGuide.summary: Call your local PACE provider for intake assessment, they handle the entire enrollment process including state certification.

steps.title: Contact PACE provider in your area
steps.description: Call the PACE provider serving your area. For Bienvivir in El Paso, the 2026 intake extension is (915) 562-3444 ext. 2300 (previously ext. 1442). For other regions, see the contact list below or call Texas 2-1-1.

steps.title: Complete eligibility assessment
steps.description: Provider conducts comprehensive health and functional assessment to determine if your loved one meets nursing home level of care criteria.

steps.title: State certification review
steps.description: The State of Texas reviews the application and certifies the nursing home level of care requirement. This typically takes 30–120 days.

steps.title: Finalize enrollment
steps.description: Once certified, provider coordinates Medicare/Medicaid enrollment if needed and schedules care team meeting to begin services.

applicationGuide.waitlist: Possible, some providers may have waitlists depending on capacity
applicationGuide.tip: PACE providers handle most of the paperwork and state certification process for you, they're motivated to help because they want to enroll eligible participants.

documentsNeeded: Valid government-issued photo ID or birth certificate for age verification
Proof of residency in PACE service area (utility bill, lease agreement, or voter registration)
Medicare card (both Part A and Part B)
Medicaid card if already enrolled
Complete medical records from primary care physician
Hospital discharge summaries from recent admissions
Current medication list with dosages and prescribing doctors
Documentation of current home care or adult day services
Assessment reports from physical therapy, occupational therapy, or speech therapy
Social Security award letter or other income documentation
Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months) if applying for Medicaid
Life insurance policies with cash values
Property deeds or mortgage statements
Vehicle titles and registration

contacts.label: Bienvivir PACE (El Paso)
contacts.description: El Paso PACE provider. The 2026 intake/enrollment extension is 2300 (previously 1442).

contacts.label: Texas 2-1-1
contacts.description: Statewide help for finding community resources, food assistance, and emergency senior services.

contacts.label: CMS PACE Information
contacts.description: General Medicare and PACE program information

contacts.label: Texas HHS / Area Agency on Aging
contacts.description: Official state resource to find PACE providers and other senior benefits in any Texas county.

applicationNotes: Most participants are dually eligible for Medicare and Medicaid, the PACE provider can help determine Medicaid eligibility if your loved one doesn't already have it
Your loved one must disenroll from Medicare Advantage or Medicare prescription drug plans before starting PACE, the provider will guide you through this process
PACE providers conduct the nursing home level of care assessment as part of enrollment, you don't need to get this done separately
Private pay is possible if your loved one needs comprehensive care coordination but doesn't qualify for Medicaid, though costs can be substantial

faqs.question: Can my loved one keep seeing their current doctors under PACE?
faqs.answer: No, PACE uses its own network of providers and care team. Your loved one will be assigned new primary care physicians, specialists, and therapists within the PACE program. All care is coordinated through the PACE team to ensure comprehensive management.

faqs.question: What if my loved one lives outside El Paso, are there other PACE programs in Texas?
faqs.answer: Yes. While PACE is only available in specific ZIP codes, multiple regional providers serve Texas, including Bienvivir (El Paso), Silver Star PACE (Lubbock), The Village PACE (Amarillo), Inspire PACE (San Antonio), and Legacy PACE (Houston). If your loved one lives outside these areas, they may instead qualify for the Texas Medicaid STAR+PLUS program or a Home and Community-based Services (HCS) waiver.

faqs.question: Can my loved one try PACE and switch back to regular Medicare if they don't like it?
faqs.answer: Yes, PACE enrollment is voluntary and your loved one can disenroll at any time. They would return to original Medicare and could re-enroll in Medicare Advantage or prescription drug plans during the next open enrollment period.

faqs.question: How much does PACE cost if my loved one has Medicare but not Medicaid?
faqs.answer: PACE accepts private pay participants, but specific costs aren't published. The program is designed to be cost-neutral to nursing home care, so expect substantial monthly fees. Contact the provider directly at (915) 562-3444 ext. 2300 for pricing.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one's health improves and they no longer need nursing home level care?
faqs.answer: PACE conducts regular reassessments. If your loved one no longer meets the nursing home level of care criteria, they would need to transition out of PACE and return to regular Medicare. The care team helps plan this transition.

faqs.question: Can I apply for PACE while my loved one is in the hospital?
faqs.answer: Yes, hospital discharge planners often initiate PACE referrals for patients who need comprehensive care coordination upon discharge. This can help ensure continuity of care when leaving the hospital.

faqs.question: Does PACE provide transportation to medical appointments?
faqs.answer: Yes, PACE includes transportation to the adult day health center and medical appointments within the program. This is part of the comprehensive service package with no additional charge for participants with Medicaid.

faqs.question: What if my loved one needs emergency care or hospitalization while in PACE?
faqs.answer: PACE covers all medical care including emergency services and hospitalizations. The PACE care team coordinates with hospitals and manages all aspects of emergency care to ensure continuity when your loved one returns home.

programs.name: Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP)
programs.tagline: Up to $298/month on an EBT card (Lone Star Card) to help your loved one buy groceries if they're 60+ with limited income

localEntities.name: Local HHSC offices (locate via 211 or YourTexasBenefits.com)

programs.intro:
If your loved one is 60+ and struggling to afford groceries on Social Security or a fixed income, SNAP provides monthly funds loaded onto an EBT card (Lone Star Card) for food purchases. In Texas, seniors can receive up to $298/month for a single person ($516 for couples), with the average senior getting around $188/month.

Because your loved one is 60+, they only need to pass the net income test (after deducting medical expenses over $35/month and high shelter costs), making qualification easier than for younger adults. Income limit: $1,305/month for one person or $1,766/month for two people after deductions. Social Security, pensions, and disability benefits count as income, but your loved one can still qualify if they have significant medical or housing expenses.

Texas requires an asset test ($4,500 limit for households with a member aged 60+ or with a disability), but this exempts their primary home, most vehicles, household goods, and some retirement savings. The Lone Star Card (EBT) works like a debit card at grocery stores but cannot be used for alcohol, tobacco, or hot prepared foods.
programs.savingsSource: Average senior benefit of $188/month to maximum benefit of $298/month

summary: Assets under $4,500 (households with a member 60+ or disabled)
otherRequirements: Must live in Texas
Include all household members who share food
Medical expenses over $35/month are deductible for seniors

applicationGuide.summary: Apply online at YourTexasBenefits.com or call 2-1-1 for assistance, processing typically takes 30 days.

steps.title: Start your application
steps.description: Apply online at YourTexasBenefits.com or call 2-1-1 to request a paper application be mailed to you.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect proof of income (Social Security award letter, pension statements), bank statements, rent/mortgage documents, and medical expense receipts over $35/month.

steps.title: Submit application and documents
steps.description: Upload documents online, mail to your local HHSC office, or drop off in person. Call 2-1-1 to find your local office.

steps.title: Complete interview
steps.description: HHSC will schedule a phone interview to review your application, typically within 7-10 days of submission.

steps.title: Receive decision
steps.description: You'll get approval or denial within 30 days. If approved, your EBT card arrives by mail within 7-10 business days.

applicationGuide.processingTime: 30 days standard, 7 days expedited if income under $150/month and assets under $100
applicationGuide.tip: Seniors get expedited processing if facing immediate food insecurity, mention this during your interview if applicable.

documentsNeeded: Valid photo ID (driver's license or state ID)
Social Security card
Social Security award letter or benefits statement
Pension or retirement income statements
Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months)
Rent receipt or mortgage statement
Utility bills (electric, gas, water)
Medical expense receipts over $35/month (prescriptions, Medicare premiums, medical bills)
Proof of citizenship or qualified immigrant status

contacts.label: Texas 2-1-1
contacts.description: Statewide helpline for SNAP applications and local office locations

contacts.label: HHSC Customer Service
contacts.description: Questions about existing SNAP benefits and case status

applicationNotes: Medical expense deductions are crucial for seniors, include Medicare premiums, prescription costs, and out-of-pocket medical bills over $35/month
If your loved one lives with family but buys and prepares food separately, they can apply as a household of one
Expedited processing available if monthly income is under $150 and assets under $100, or if facing utility shutoff
Social Security and pension income count toward income limits, but high medical or shelter costs can reduce net income below limits
relatedPrograms: Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)

faqs.question: Can my loved one keep their house if it's worth more than $4,500?
faqs.answer: Yes. The primary home where your loved one lives is completely exempt from SNAP asset limits, regardless of its value. For households with a member aged 60 or older, the countable asset limit is $4,500 (not $2,000). Texas primarily counts liquid assets like bank accounts and stocks toward this limit.

faqs.question: Does Social Security income disqualify my loved one from SNAP?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. Social Security counts as income, but seniors get to deduct medical expenses over $35/month and high shelter costs first. If your loved one has significant medical bills or housing costs, their net income may fall below the $1,305/month limit even with Social Security.

faqs.question: Can I apply for SNAP on behalf of my elderly loved one?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members can apply as an authorized representative if your loved one cannot apply themselves due to age, disability, or other barriers. You'll need to complete additional paperwork during the application process to establish this relationship.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one's income changes after approval?
faqs.answer: Report income changes to HHSC within 10 days by calling (877) 541-7905 or logging into YourTexasBenefits.com. Benefits are recertified every 36 months for seniors, and HHSC will adjust the benefit amount based on new income information.

faqs.question: Can my loved one use SNAP benefits to buy groceries online?
faqs.answer: Yes, Texas participates in SNAP online purchasing. Your loved one can use their EBT card to buy eligible groceries for delivery or pickup from participating retailers like Amazon, Walmart, and H-E-B through their websites or apps. Important for 2026: as of April 1, 2026, Texas law prohibits using SNAP benefits to buy candy or sweetened drinks (like soda), whether shopping online or in-person.

faqs.question: What if my loved one owns a car worth more than $4,500?
faqs.answer: One vehicle per household is exempt regardless of value if used for transportation. Additional vehicles may count toward the asset limit, but Texas has exceptions for work vehicles, vehicles needed for medical transportation, and vehicles being used to produce income.

programs.name: Low-Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)
programs.tagline: Get $1,000–$12,600 paid directly to your loved one's utility company to cover heating and cooling bills.

localEntities.name: Local CAAs via 211Texas.org or (877) 399-8939; full subrecipient list on TDHCA site

programs.intro: If your loved one struggles to pay electric, gas, or propane bills on a fixed income, LIHEAP provides direct payments to utility companies. In Texas, this program is delivered through CEAP (Comprehensive Energy Assistance Program) and covers one-time payments of $1,000 to $12,600 for regular heating and cooling assistance, plus up to $2,400 in crisis situations like shutoff notices.
programs.savingsSource: Texas Department of Housing and Community Affairs CEAP guidelines

summary: Household income at or below 150% FPL
otherRequirements: Texas resident with proof (utility bill, lease)
U.S. citizen or qualified non-citizen (lawful permanent resident, refugee)
Automatic eligibility if receiving SNAP, SSI, TANF, or certain veterans' benefits

applicationGuide.summary: To apply, call the TDHCA at 1-877-399-8939 (weekdays, 8 a.m. – 5 p.m.) or dial 2-1-1 to find your local agency. Note that waitlists and processing times vary by county.

steps.title: Find your local agency
steps.description: Call 2-1-1 or (877) 399-8939 to locate your Community Action Agency or CEAP provider. You can also search at 211Texas.org.

steps.title: Gather required documents
steps.description: Collect proof of income, identification, recent utility bills in the applicant's name, and proof of Texas residency before contacting the agency.

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Apply by phone, online, mail, or in-person through your local CAA. Each agency has different application methods and forms.

steps.title: Wait for caseworker contact
steps.description: You'll be placed on a waitlist. A caseworker will call when your turn arrives and funding is available for your area.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Varies by region; placed on waitlist after submission, caseworker calls when turn arrives
applicationGuide.waitlist: Yes, common; applications placed in line by site, funding-dependent
applicationGuide.tip: If you have an online account with your utility company (like Texas Gas Service), set it up before applying, it speeds up the documentation process.

documentsNeeded: Valid government-issued photo ID (Texas driver's license, passport, or state ID)
Proof of citizenship or legal residency status for all household members
Proof of income from last 30 days (pay stubs, Social Security award letter, pension statements)
Most recent electric bill in applicant's name
Most recent gas or propane bill in applicant's name
Proof of Texas residency (utility bill, lease agreement, or mortgage statement)
Names and dates of birth for all household members sharing the address
SNAP, SSI, TANF, or veterans' benefits award letters if applicable
Shutoff notice or past-due utility bill if applying for crisis assistance

contacts.label: Texas 2-1-1
contacts.description: Free statewide helpline to find your local CEAP agency

contacts.label: LIHEAP National Helpline
contacts.description: Federal helpline for energy assistance programs

contacts.label: Travis County CEAP
contacts.description: Example local agency serving Austin area

applicationNotes: Households facing a life-threatening energy crisis (such as a broken AC in extreme heat or an immediate shutoff) may receive expedited processing within 48 hours, even when standard waitlists are long.
Household size includes everyone living at the address who shares utility bills, not just family members
Benefits are paid directly to your utility company, you won't receive a check or cash payment
Funding runs out quickly each year, so apply as early as possible when applications open

contentSections.title: Crisis Assistance Available
contentSections.content: If your loved one received a shutoff notice or is facing an energy emergency, mention this when applying. Crisis assistance provides up to $2,400 in emergency payments and may receive faster processing, though you'll still join a waitlist.

faqs.question: How long is the waitlist really?
faqs.answer: Wait times vary significantly by county and available funding. Some areas process applications within 4–6 weeks, while others have waitlists lasting several months. Crisis cases with shutoff notices typically receive priority and can often be resolved within 48 hours, but standard applications face delays when federal funding is low.

faqs.question: Can I apply on behalf of my elderly loved one?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members can apply on behalf of elderly or disabled parents. You'll need to provide all required documentation and may need to sign as their authorized representative with the local Community Action Agency.

faqs.question: What if my loved one's income is slightly over the limit?
faqs.answer: The 2026 gross monthly income limit for a single person is $1,995 (150% of the Federal Poverty Level). If they exceed this, they generally do not qualify. However, if they receive SNAP, SSI, or TANF, they are often "categorically eligible," which simplifies the income verification process.

faqs.question: Can my loved one get LIHEAP and apply for other energy programs at the same time?
faqs.answer: Yes, LIHEAP doesn't prevent eligibility for other programs. They can also apply for the Weatherization Assistance Program, which provides free home energy improvements, and utility company assistance programs simultaneously.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one moves to a different county after applying?
faqs.answer: Each county has its own Community Action Agency and waitlist. If your loved one moves, they'll need to start over with a new application in their new county. Benefits don't transfer between agencies.

faqs.question: Does the program cover propane or only electric and gas?
faqs.answer: LIHEAP covers all home heating and cooling costs, including propane, fuel oil, wood, coal, and electricity. You'll need to provide recent bills for whatever energy source your loved one uses for heating or cooling.

faqs.question: What if my loved one rents and utilities are included in rent?
faqs.answer: If utilities are included in rent, your loved one typically isn't eligible since they don't receive direct utility bills. However, some agencies may accept a landlord statement showing the utility portion of rent. Contact your local CAA to ask about their specific policy.

faqs.question: How often can my loved one receive LIHEAP benefits?
faqs.answer: In Texas, eligible households can typically receive assistance for 6 to 8 utility payments per program year, depending on their vulnerability status (seniors and those with disabilities often qualify for the full 8 payments). This is not a "one-time check" but ongoing support throughout the year until their maximum benefit is reached.

programs.name: Weatherization Assistance Program
programs.tagline: Free home energy efficiency improvements that can cut your loved one's utility bills by hundreds of dollars annually.

localEntities.name: Examples: TCOG (Sherman), SPCAA (South Plains), ATCog, Dallas County HHS, Austin Energy, CCSCT (Uvalde region counties), Oncor low-income weatherization[9].

programs.intro:
If your loved one's household income is at or below 200% of the federal poverty level, they may qualify for free home weatherization services that can significantly reduce monthly utility bills. The program provides a professional energy audit followed by improvements like insulation installation, air leak sealing, duct work repairs, and heating/cooling system tune-ups or replacements.

Because Texas administers this program through approximately 30 regional agencies rather than one state office, you'll need to contact the specific subrecipient that serves your loved one's county. Priority goes to households with elderly residents, people with disabilities, or families with children under 6, though waitlists are common due to funding limits.

summary: Household income at or below 200% federal poverty level
U.S. citizen or qualified alien status for all household members
Single-family home, duplex, triplex, fourplex, or eligible mobile home
In most Texas regions, the property must not have received WAP services in the last 15 years.
Both homeowners and renters qualify, though renters must obtain signed permission from their landlord.
otherRequirements: Home must pass energy audit to confirm need for weatherization
Property must not have been weatherized in the last 15 years in some regions
Renters need landlord permission
Some regions have property value limits (e.g., $478,195 or less excluding land in Austin)
Mobile homes are excluded in some regions but allowed in others

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your regional subrecipient agency by phone or mail, processing times vary widely by region and funding availability.

steps.title: Find your regional agency
steps.description: Texas divides weatherization by region. Examples: TCOG serves North Texas (800-677-8264 ext. 3530), Austin Energy serves Austin area (512-482-5346), CCSCT serves Uvalde region counties. Check the full subrecipient list at TDHCA's website.

steps.title: Gather income documentation
steps.description: Collect proof of income from the last 30 days only for all household members age 18 and older. This includes pay stubs, Social Security award letters, unemployment documents, pension letters. Bank statements and tax returns are not accepted.

steps.title: Submit application
steps.description: Complete your regional agency's specific application form. You can apply by mail, in-person, or online in some areas like Austin Energy. Include all required documentation for every household member.

steps.title: Wait for eligibility determination
steps.description: You'll receive an eligibility memo by mail after processing. Due to high demand, most regions have waitlists and use priority scoring that favors elderly, disabled, or families with young children.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Varies widely by region, number of clients, and funding availability, no fixed statewide timeline
applicationGuide.waitlist: Common due to funding limits; prioritization matrix used (e.g., points for elderly, disabled, children under 6)
applicationGuide.tip: Apply even if you think there might be a waitlist, priority scoring often helps elderly households move up the list faster.

documentsNeeded: Proof of income for all household members 18+ from last 30 days (pay stubs, Social Security award letters, unemployment documents, pension letters, workers comp, TANF)
Notarized Declaration of Income Statement if anyone had no income in the last 30 days
Birth certificates for all household members
Naturalization or citizenship certificates for non-U.S. born members
U.S. passports or other proof of citizenship/qualified alien status
Driver's license or state ID for all household members 18+
Current electric bill with 12-month usage history
Current gas or propane bill with 12-month usage history
Landlord Permission Form (completed and signed if renting)
Names and dates of birth for all household members
Proof of primary residence (lease agreement, property deed, or utility bills)

contacts.label: TCOG (North Texas)
contacts.description: Weatherization services for North Texas region

contacts.label: Austin Energy
contacts.description: Austin area weatherization program

contacts.label: Texas 2-1-1
contacts.description: Free helpline to find your regional weatherization agency

applicationNotes: You must contact the correct regional subrecipient for your county, the state does not accept direct applications
Income documentation is strictly limited to the last 30 days and gets annualized, older pay stubs or tax returns will not be accepted
Every household member needs citizenship verification, including children, which can delay applications if documents are missing
If your loved one is elderly, disabled, or lives with young children, mention this when applying as it may improve their priority score
relatedPrograms: Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)

faqs.question: What if my loved one's mobile home isn't eligible in their region?
faqs.answer: Mobile home eligibility varies by regional agency. Some exclude them entirely (like ATCog), while others include them. Call your regional subrecipient directly to confirm whether mobile homes qualify in your area, as this rule isn't consistent statewide.

faqs.question: Can I apply on behalf of my elderly loved one?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members can help with the application process. However, your loved one will need to sign forms and provide their personal documentation. Some agencies may require your loved one to be present for the initial energy audit scheduling.

faqs.question: How long is the waitlist really?
faqs.answer: Waitlists vary dramatically by region and funding cycles. Some areas process applications within months, while others have year-long waits. However, households with elderly members, people with disabilities, or children under 6 get priority points that can significantly reduce wait times.

faqs.question: What if the landlord won't sign the permission form?
faqs.answer: Unfortunately, weatherization services cannot proceed without a signed Landlord Permission Form. The program requires access to the property for an audit and contractor work. If the landlord refuses, your loved one may still qualify for utility bill assistance through the CEAP program, which often does not require physical home modifications.

faqs.question: Can my loved one apply if they received weatherization services in the past?
faqs.answer: In most Texas regions, a property is ineligible if it has been weatherized by WAP within the last 15 years. If the previous service was more than 15 years ago, they may be eligible to apply again for updated energy-efficiency measures.

faqs.question: What happens if the energy audit shows my loved one's home doesn't need weatherization?
faqs.answer: No services will be provided if the audit determines the home doesn't meet energy-savings requirements. The audit itself is free, but weatherization work only proceeds if it meets Department of Energy efficiency standards for cost-effectiveness.

faqs.question: Can my loved one apply for both weatherization and LIHEAP at the same time?
faqs.answer: Yes, these are separate programs and you can apply for both simultaneously. In fact, some regional agencies coordinate these services or may suggest applying for LIHEAP while on the weatherization waitlist for immediate utility bill help.

faqs.question: What if my loved one's income includes irregular Social Security or pension payments?
faqs.answer: You must provide proof of all gross income received in the 30 days prior to the application date. If a regular payment was missed or an extra one was received in that window, provide the official Award Letter from the Social Security Administration or pension source to help the agency accurately annualize the income.

programs.name: Texas State Health Insurance Assistance Program (SHIP)
programs.tagline: Free one-on-one help choosing Medicare plans and applying for prescription drug savings.

localEntities.name: Local SHIP sites via statewide network; contact 1-800-252-9240 for nearest

programs.intro:
If your loved one is on Medicare or turning 65, Texas SHIP provides free counseling to help them choose the right health and prescription drug plans. Because Medicare has dozens of plan options that change every year, a trained counselor can review your loved one's specific medications and doctors to find the plan that saves the most money.

SHIP counselors also help apply for Medicare Savings Programs and Extra Help (Low-Income Subsidy) that can reduce prescription drug costs to $4-$10 per month. In Texas, this program is called the Health Information, Counseling, and Advocacy Program (HICAP). Anyone with Medicare can use this service regardless of income, no eligibility requirements beyond having Medicare.

summary: Must have Medicare or be turning 65
Family caregivers can get help too
otherRequirements: Must be Medicare-eligible (typically age 65+ or under 65 with certain disabilities)
Family members and caregivers also eligible for counseling

applicationGuide.summary: Call 1-800-252-9240 to schedule free counseling, typically available within days.

steps.title: Call the Texas SHIP hotline
steps.description: Call 1-800-252-9240 to find your nearest counselor and schedule an appointment. Available statewide through local community organizations.

steps.title: Gather your Medicare information
steps.description: Have your Medicare card, current health plan details, and list of medications ready for your counseling session.

steps.title: Meet with your counselor
steps.description: Counseling available in-person, by phone, or through group presentations. Sessions are free and confidential.

applicationGuide.processingTime: No formal application processing, counseling provided upon contact, typically immediate or by appointment
applicationGuide.tip: Schedule counseling during Medicare Open Enrollment (October 15 - December 7) early, as this is the busiest time.

contacts.label: Texas SHIP Hotline
contacts.description: Free Medicare counseling and plan comparison
contacts.hours: Business hours (contact for local counselor availability)

contacts.label: Medicare.gov
contacts.description: Official Medicare helpline

faqs.question: Can I get help even if my loved one already has Medicare coverage?
faqs.answer: Yes. SHIP counselors help review existing plans during Open Enrollment (October 15 - December 7) to see if switching could save money. They can also help apply for Extra Help or Medicare Savings Programs that reduce costs for current coverage.

faqs.question: What's the difference between SHIP and calling Medicare directly?
faqs.answer: SHIP counselors are local, unbiased volunteers who don't sell insurance. Because they know Texas-specific programs and can meet in person, they often catch savings opportunities that Medicare's national hotline might miss. Both services are free.

faqs.question: Can a family member call on behalf of an elderly loved one?
faqs.answer: Yes, family caregivers can get counseling about Medicare options for their loved ones. However, the Medicare beneficiary will need to be present or give permission for plan enrollment changes.

programs.name: Meals on Wheels
programs.tagline: Home-delivered meals and daily check-ins for homebound seniors 60+ across Texas.

localEntities.name: Meals on Wheels North Central Texas (Johnson/Ellis/Navarro)
Meals on Wheels of Tarrant County
Meals on Wheels Senior Services of Rockwall County

programs.intro:
If your loved one is homebound and struggling to prepare nutritious meals, Meals on Wheels delivers hot, ready-to-eat meals directly to their door on weekdays. Because volunteers also provide a brief safety check during each delivery, you get peace of mind knowing someone is checking on your loved one daily. Many programs also offer frozen weekend meals and can tailor meals for specific medical conditions like diabetes or heart disease.

The program serves anyone 60+ who is primarily homebound and unable to prepare nutritious meals without consistent help. Because there are no income limits, your loved one can qualify regardless of their financial situation, voluntary contributions are requested but no one is denied for inability to pay.

Meals on Wheels operates through independent local providers across Texas, so you must contact the specific program serving your loved one's area. Processing time varies from within a week to longer if there's a waitlist, depending on the local provider.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must be primarily homebound and unable to prepare nutritious meals without consistent daytime assistance
otherRequirements: Must reside in the specific local program's delivery zone
Must be able to accept meals during delivery window (typically 10:30am-1:00pm)
Priority given to those with greatest economic/social need or severe disabilities

applicationGuide.summary: Contact your local Meals on Wheels provider by phone or online referral form, anyone can refer including family members.

steps.title: Find your local provider
steps.description: Use the Meals on Wheels America finder at mealsonwheelsamerica.org/find-meals-and-services/ to locate the program serving your loved one's area.

steps.title: Submit referral
steps.description: Call directly or complete online referral form. Examples: Tarrant County 817-336-0912, Rockwall County 972-771-9514. Provide basic info: health problems, date of birth, SSN, doctor info, emergency contacts.

steps.title: Schedule assessment
steps.description: A case manager or social worker will contact you to schedule an in-person home visit to assess your loved one's needs and eligibility.

steps.title: Begin service
steps.description: If approved, meal delivery typically begins within days of the assessment, though some areas have waitlists.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Varies by location, some within a week, others longer if waitlist exists. Tarrant County schedules caseworker appointments within 2 working days.
applicationGuide.waitlist: Possible in some programs, leading to longer processing times
applicationGuide.tip: Anyone can make the referral, you don't need to wait for your loved one to call themselves.

documentsNeeded: Proof of age (birth certificate, driver's license, or state ID)
Proof of residence in the service area (utility bill or lease)
Medical information about dietary restrictions or special needs
Doctor's contact information
Emergency contact information
Social Security number

contacts.label: Meals on Wheels America Finder
contacts.description: National helpline and online tool to find your local provider

contacts.label: Tarrant County
contacts.description: Meals on Wheels of Tarrant County

contacts.label: Rockwall County
contacts.description: Meals on Wheels Senior Services of Rockwall County

contacts.label: Texas 2-1-1
contacts.description: Can help locate your local Meals on Wheels provider

applicationNotes: To qualify, your loved one must be homebound and unable to safely shop for groceries or cook their own meals
Short-term service is available for recovery periods but typically limited to 6 weeks
Some programs offer trial meals for a fee if you want to skip the full assessment process
Priority is often given to those with the greatest need, including dementia or severe disabilities

faqs.question: Can I apply for my loved one who lives in a different county than me?
faqs.answer: Yes, but you must contact the Meals on Wheels provider that serves your loved one's specific area, not yours. Each program only delivers within their designated service zone, so location determines which provider to call.

faqs.question: What if my loved one can still drive but has trouble cooking?
faqs.answer: Your loved one may not qualify if they can easily leave home and access other meal options. The program prioritizes those who are homebound, meaning they rarely leave home without significant assistance or difficulty.

faqs.question: How long is the waitlist in most Texas areas?
faqs.answer: Waitlist length varies dramatically by location and isn't publicly reported by most providers. Some areas start service within a week, while others have longer waits. Priority is given to those with the greatest need, so severe disabilities or dementia may move your loved one up the list.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one can't afford the suggested donation?
faqs.answer: No one is denied service for inability to pay. Donations are voluntary and requested to help sustain the program, but your loved one will receive meals regardless of whether they can contribute financially.

faqs.question: Can my loved one get meals temporarily while recovering from surgery?
faqs.answer: Yes, many programs offer short-term service for recovery periods, typically limited to about 6 weeks. Contact your local provider to discuss temporary meal delivery options during your loved one's recovery.

faqs.question: What if my loved one has special dietary needs like diabetes?
faqs.answer: Many programs can accommodate medical dietary restrictions and may offer medically-tailored meals. Provide information about your loved one's health conditions and dietary needs during the referral process so they can plan appropriate meals.

programs.name: Community Based Alternatives (CBA) Waiver
programs.tagline: Help your loved one avoid nursing home placement and receive care at home through this Texas Medicaid waiver program
programs.intro:
If your loved one needs nursing home-level care but wants to stay in their own home, the Community Based Alternatives (CBA) Waiver can make that possible. This Texas Medicaid program pays for personal care attendants, home modifications, respite care, and nursing services, essentially bringing nursing home services to your loved one's house. Your loved one must qualify for Medicaid and meet nursing facility level of care (meaning they need daily help with activities like bathing, dressing, or medication management).

Because this waiver covers comprehensive home care services under one coordinated plan, your loved one won't need to piece together care from multiple providers, one team manages everything from personal attendants to therapy services. The total cost of your loved one's service plan cannot exceed what Texas would pay for nursing home care, but there's no fixed dollar limit or hour cap, services are tailored to their specific needs.

There is a waitlist for this program. Wait times vary by region and your loved one's priority level, with those at highest risk of nursing home placement typically served first. Income limit: $2,829/month for a single person (2025). Asset limit: $2,000 for an individual, $3,000 for a couple.

structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Must meet nursing facility level of care criteria (needing daily assistance with bathing, dressing, eating, mobility, or medication management) and be at risk for nursing home placement
otherRequirements: Must qualify for Medicaid
Must choose waiver services over nursing facility care
Cannot already be in a nursing facility
Texas resident

applicationGuide.summary: Apply for Medicaid first through Your Texas Benefits online portal, then contact local providers for waiver services, total process typically takes 45 days.

steps.title: Apply for Medicaid
steps.description: Visit yourtexasbenefits.com or call 2-1-1 to apply for Medicaid using Form H1200. You must qualify for Medicaid before accessing waiver services.

steps.title: Request waiver interest list
steps.description: Call HHSC at (877) 541-7905 or contact local providers to be added to the CBA waiver interest list. This step can be done simultaneously with Medicaid application.

steps.title: Complete functional assessment
steps.description: Once called from the waitlist, a clinical team will assess whether your loved one meets nursing facility level of care requirements through in-person evaluation.

steps.title: Develop service plan
steps.description: If approved, work with the assigned care coordinator to create an individualized service plan that cannot exceed the cost of nursing home care.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Medicaid eligibility determination typically 45 days, but waiver interest list placement is immediate
applicationGuide.waitlist: Yes, waitlists are common and can extend from several months to multiple years depending on region and priority level
applicationGuide.tip: Apply to the interest list as soon as you think your loved one might need services, even if they're not ready now, you can decline when called if the timing isn't right.

documentsNeeded: Social Security cards for applicant and spouse
Medicare card (both parts A and B)
Proof of age (birth certificate or passport)
Proof of Texas residency (utility bill, lease, or state-issued document)
Most recent Social Security award letter
Pension or retirement income statements
Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months)
Investment documents (stocks, bonds, annuities)
Property documents (deeds, tax statements)
Life insurance policies with face values
Vehicle titles and registration
Medical records showing functional limitations
Physician statement regarding need for nursing facility level of care
Proof of health insurance premiums paid
Pre-need burial contracts if applicable

contacts.label: Texas 2-1-1
contacts.description: Free 24/7 helpline for all social services including CBA waiver information

contacts.label: HHSC Benefits Line
contacts.description: Medicaid and waiver program questions

applicationNotes: You can apply for Medicaid and request waiver interest list placement simultaneously, don't wait for Medicaid approval to get on the waitlist
Wait times vary significantly by region, urban areas like Houston and Dallas typically have longer waits than rural areas
Priority is given to those at highest risk of nursing home placement, emergency situations may receive expedited consideration
Your loved one cannot be enrolled in both CBA waiver and nursing facility care at the same time, it's an either/or choice

faqs.question: Can my loved one keep their house if it's worth more than the asset limit?
faqs.answer: Yes, your loved one's primary home is exempt from the $2,000 asset limit as long as they live there or intend to return, and the home equity doesn't exceed $730,000. The home remains exempt even if your spouse or disabled child lives there.

faqs.question: How long is the waitlist really?
faqs.answer: Wait times vary from several months to multiple years depending on your region and your loved one's priority level. Urban areas like Houston and Dallas typically have longer waits than rural areas. Those at highest risk of immediate nursing home placement get priority.

faqs.question: Can I apply on behalf of my elderly loved one?
faqs.answer: Yes, you can apply as your loved one's authorized representative if they cannot apply themselves due to physical or cognitive limitations. You'll need to complete additional paperwork establishing your authority to act on their behalf.

faqs.question: What if my loved one's income is slightly over the $2,829 limit?
faqs.answer: Your loved one may still qualify through a Medicaid spend-down process, where they pay the excess income toward medical expenses each month. Contact HHSC at (877) 541-7905 to discuss spend-down options for your loved one's specific situation.

faqs.question: Can my loved one receive CBA waiver services and still live in assisted living?
faqs.answer: Yes, CBA waiver services can be provided in various community settings including assisted living facilities, as long as your loved one isn't in a nursing facility. The services complement rather than replace the assisted living care.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one's needs increase after they're enrolled?
faqs.answer: The care coordinator will reassess your loved one's needs and can modify the service plan accordingly, as long as the total cost doesn't exceed the nursing facility payment rate. Regular reassessments ensure services match current needs.

faqs.question: Can my loved one choose their own personal care attendant?
faqs.answer: In many cases, yes. Your loved one may be able to hire family members or friends as personal attendants, though specific rules vary by the managed care organization. The attendant must meet training and background check requirements.

faqs.question: What if we move to a different part of Texas after enrollment?
faqs.answer: Your loved one can maintain CBA waiver services when moving within Texas, but you'll need to transfer to the new region's STAR+PLUS managed care organization. Contact your current care coordinator to initiate the transfer process before moving.

programs.name: Community Caregiver Support Program
programs.tagline: Get help coordinating care for your aging loved one through local support centers across Texas.

localEntities.name: Local Aging and Disability Resource Centers (ADRCs)

programs.intro:
If your loved one needs help with daily tasks like bathing, meal preparation, or getting to appointments, Texas's community caregiver programs can connect you to local services and support. These programs work through your local Aging and Disability Resource Center (ADRC) to help older adults stay in their homes longer.

The main program, Community Care for Aged/Disabled (CCAD), provides non-medical support services for people who need help with everyday activities but don't qualify for full Medicaid. For the 2026 fiscal year, the gross income limits are $2,982/month for a single person and $5,964/month for couples. Asset limits are $5,000 for individuals and $6,000 for couples.

Because services are coordinated through local ADRCs, your loved one gets one point of contact instead of calling multiple agencies. Wait times and available services vary by region depending on local capacity.
programs.savingsSource: Free service coordination and connection to local resources

summary: Age 18+ with disability or daily care needs
structuredEligibility.functionalRequirement: Need help with daily activities like bathing, dressing, meal preparation, or household tasks
otherRequirements: Texas residency required
Cannot be receiving Medicaid for the same services
Must be 18 or older (those under 60 must have a verified disability)

applicationGuide.summary: Call your local Aging and Disability Resource Center at 1-855-937-2372 to start the process.

steps.title: Call the statewide ADRC line
steps.description: Call 1-855-937-2372 to be connected to your local Aging and Disability Resource Center. They'll assess your loved one's needs over the phone.

steps.title: Complete intake assessment
steps.description: Answer questions about your loved one's daily living needs, income, assets, and current living situation. This helps determine which services they qualify for.

steps.title: Provide required documentation
steps.description: Submit proof of income, Texas residency, and care needs. Your local ADRC will tell you exactly what documents they need.

steps.title: Wait for service coordination
steps.description: Your ADRC will match your loved one with available local providers. Wait times vary by region and service type.

applicationGuide.waitlist: Varies by region and service type
applicationGuide.tip: Call early in the day (before 10am) when ADRC staff have more time for detailed conversations about your loved one's specific needs.

documentsNeeded: Valid government-issued photo ID for your loved one
Proof of Texas residency (utility bill, lease, or driver's license)
Social Security award letter or pension statements
Bank statements for all accounts (last 3 months)
Medicare card and any other health insurance cards
List of current medications and medical conditions

contacts.label: Aging and Disability Resource Centers
contacts.description: Statewide line connecting to local ADRCs for caregiver support services

contacts.label: Texas Health and Human Services
contacts.description: General benefits and Medicaid questions

applicationNotes: Program names can be confusing, when you call, ask specifically about 'Community Care for Aged and Disabled' or 'CCAD' services
Some Texas counties have immediate openings, while others maintain a "priority-based" waitlist. When you call, ask: "What is the current wait time for services in my specific ZIP code?"
Income and asset limits are adjusted annually, so 2025 limits may be slightly higher than current figures

faqs.question: Can I apply on behalf of my elderly loved one?
faqs.answer: Yes, adult children can initiate the application process by calling the ADRC. However, your loved one will need to participate in the assessment interview, or you'll need legal authority (power of attorney) to act on their behalf. The ADRC staff will guide you through what's required.

faqs.question: What if my loved one's income is slightly over the limit?
faqs.answer: For 2026, the gross income limits are $2,982/month for single people and $5,964 for couples. If your loved one is over these amounts, they may still qualify for other programs like STAR+PLUS or private-pay services coordinated through the ADRC. Don't assume they're ineligible without calling.

faqs.question: How long is the waitlist really?
faqs.answer: Wait times vary significantly by region and service type. Rural areas often have shorter waits but fewer service options. Urban areas may have longer waits but more providers. When you call your ADRC, ask for honest wait time estimates for the specific services your loved one needs.

faqs.question: Can my loved one get both this program and Medicaid services?
faqs.answer: No, your loved one cannot receive CCAD services and Medicaid services for the same needs. However, they might qualify for STAR+PLUS (Medicaid waiver) instead, which provides more comprehensive coverage. Your ADRC will help determine which program offers better benefits for your loved one's situation.

faqs.question: What happens if my loved one's needs change after enrollment?
faqs.answer: The ADRC will reassess your loved one's needs periodically, usually annually or when you request it. If their care needs increase, they may qualify for additional services or need to transition to a different program like STAR+PLUS. Call your ADRC whenever your loved one's situation changes significantly.

faqs.question: Does my loved one's house count toward the asset limit?
faqs.answer: No, your loved one's primary home is exempt from the $5,000 individual or $6,000 couple asset limits. One vehicle is also exempt. The limits apply mainly to bank accounts, investments, and cash. If your loved one has significant non-exempt assets, ask the ADRC about spend-down options.

programs.name: Legal Aid for Seniors through AAAs
programs.tagline: Free legal help for Texas seniors 60+ with Medicare, housing, and consumer issues through local Area Agencies on Aging.

localEntities.name: 28 AAAs including Concho Valley AAA (San Angelo), West Central Texas AAA (Abilene), Capital Area AAA (Austin area), Area Agency on Aging of Central Texas (Belton)

programs.intro:
If your loved one is 60+ and facing legal issues with Medicare benefits, nursing home complaints, or housing problems, Texas's 28 Area Agencies on Aging provide free legal counseling and advocacy. Because these services are provided through AAAs rather than traditional legal aid organizations, your loved one can get help regardless of income level, though priority goes to those with the greatest need.

The program covers civil legal matters only: Medicare and Medicaid benefits counseling, ombudsman advocacy for nursing homes and assisted living facilities, consumer protection issues, and housing disputes. Your loved one will get one-on-one counseling from trained staff, plus referrals to specialized legal aid organizations when needed. Because all 254 Texas counties are covered by the 28 regional AAAs, there's a local office that serves your loved one's area.

otherRequirements: Texas resident in county served by one of 28 AAAs
Civil legal matters only (no criminal or personal injury cases)
Priority given to those with functional impairment, isolation, or low income

applicationGuide.summary: Call your local AAA directly for immediate counseling or referral, no forms required.

steps.title: Find your local AAA
steps.description: Texas has 28 regional AAAs covering all counties. Examples: Concho Valley AAA serves San Angelo area at (325) 223-5704, West Central Texas AAA serves Abilene area at (325) 672-8544, Capital Area AAA serves Austin area at (512) 916-6062.

steps.title: Call for intake screening
steps.description: Explain your loved one's legal issue during the phone call. Staff will provide immediate counseling for benefits questions or nursing home advocacy, or refer to appropriate legal aid organizations for complex matters.

steps.title: Receive services or referral
steps.description: AAA staff provide direct ombudsman services and benefits counseling. For cases requiring legal representation, they refer to organizations like Legal Aid of NorthWest Texas (325) 677-8591 or Texas RioGrande Legal Aid (512) 374-2700.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Immediate counseling or referral upon contact
applicationGuide.tip: Contact the AAA first, not the legal aid organizations directly, AAA staff can often resolve benefits and nursing home issues without needing a lawyer.

documentsNeeded: Valid ID showing age 60+
Proof of Texas residency
Details of legal issue (Medicare statements, nursing home complaints, housing notices)
Income information if requesting priority services
Proof of participation in SNAP, Medicaid, or SSI for legal aid referrals
Any relevant legal documents related to the issue

contacts.label: Statewide AAA Referral
contacts.description: Texas HHS routing line. You will be automatically routed to the Area Agency on Aging serving your specific county.

contacts.label: Concho Valley AAA
contacts.description: Serves San Angelo and surrounding counties

contacts.label: West Central Texas AAA
contacts.description: Serves Abilene and surrounding counties

contacts.label: Capital Area AAA
contacts.description: Serves Austin area and surrounding counties

contacts.label: Legal Aid of NorthWest Texas
contacts.description: Legal representation referrals for North/West Texas

faqs.question: What's the difference between AAA legal services and regular legal aid?
faqs.answer: AAA legal services have no income limits and focus on counseling, benefits help, and nursing home advocacy. Traditional legal aid organizations like Legal Aid of NorthWest Texas require income below 125-200% of Federal Poverty Guidelines but provide full legal representation in court. AAAs often refer to legal aid when court representation is needed.

faqs.question: Can I call on behalf of my loved one who has dementia?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members and caregivers can contact AAAs on behalf of seniors. You'll need details about the legal issue and may need to provide documentation showing your authority to act (like power of attorney) depending on the situation.

faqs.question: Will my loved one get priority if they're low-income?
faqs.answer: Yes, AAAs prioritize services for seniors with the greatest need, including those with low income, functional impairments, social isolation, or high nutritional risk. However, services are available to all eligible seniors regardless of income level.

faqs.question: What if the legal issue involves criminal charges or a personal injury lawsuit?
faqs.answer: AAAs only handle civil legal matters like benefits, housing, and consumer issues. For criminal defense or personal injury cases, they'll refer you to appropriate legal resources, but these fall outside their scope of services.

programs.name: Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program
programs.tagline: Free advocacy and complaint resolution when your loved one faces problems in their nursing home or assisted living facility.

localEntities.name: Local programs via ltco.texas.gov 'Find an LTC Ombudsman' tool; examples: Cizik School of Nursing (Harris County)[3], Texoma COG[4]

programs.intro:
If your loved one lives in a Texas nursing home or assisted living facility and faces problems with care, billing, or treatment, the Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program provides free advocacy to resolve complaints and protect their rights. Ombudsmen investigate issues like medication errors, dignity violations, financial exploitation, or discharge disputes, and they work independently from the facilities.

The service is completely free with no income limits or paperwork required. Residents of any age (not just those 60+) and their family members can receive assistance navigating facility issues. Because ombudsmen are trained advocates who know facility regulations, they can often resolve problems that families struggle with alone.

Every complaint is kept confidential, and ombudsmen can represent your loved one in hearings, pursue remedies with facility management, and ensure access to medical records and inspection reports.

summary: Resident of nursing home or assisted living in Texas
No age, income, or asset limits
Family members of residents 60+ also eligible
otherRequirements: Must be a resident of a nursing facility or assisted living facility in Texas, or a family member of a resident age 60+

applicationGuide.summary: Call 800-252-2412 to speak with a local ombudsman immediately, no application or paperwork required.

steps.title: Call the statewide hotline
steps.description: Call 800-252-2412 to be connected with your local Long-Term Care Ombudsman. Available during business hours for immediate assistance.

steps.title: Provide facility and concern details
steps.description: Give the ombudsman your loved one's name, facility name and location, and specific details about the problem or complaint.

steps.title: Ombudsman investigates
steps.description: The ombudsman will visit the facility, review records, interview staff, and work to resolve the issue while keeping your complaint confidential.

applicationGuide.processingTime: Immediate response for complaints and advocacy
applicationGuide.tip: You can also find your local ombudsman online at ltco.texas.gov using the 'Find an LTC Ombudsman' tool if you prefer to contact them directly.

contacts.label: Statewide LTC Ombudsman Hotline
contacts.description: Connects you with local ombudsman for complaints and advocacy
contacts.hours: Business hours, varies by local office

contacts.label: Texas 2-1-1
contacts.description: General helpline for all social services including LTC resources

faqs.question: Can I file a complaint on behalf of my loved one who has dementia?
faqs.answer: Yes, family members can file complaints and request ombudsman assistance for residents who cannot advocate for themselves. The ombudsman will still visit your loved one and assess their situation directly, but they understand that family involvement is often necessary for residents with cognitive impairments.

faqs.question: Will the facility retaliate against my loved one if I file a complaint?
faqs.answer: Retaliation is illegal and ombudsmen specifically monitor for it. All complaints are kept confidential, and ombudsmen are trained to protect residents from reprisal. If retaliation occurs, contact your ombudsman immediately, they have authority to pursue remedies and escalate to regulatory agencies.

faqs.question: What if my loved one wants to leave the facility but the facility won't discharge them?
faqs.answer: Ombudsmen can help with discharge disputes and transfer issues. They know facility regulations about discharge procedures and can advocate for your loved one's right to leave when medically appropriate. Call 800-252-2412 to discuss the specific circumstances.

faqs.question: Does the ombudsman program help with billing or payment disputes?
faqs.answer: Yes, ombudsmen handle financial complaints including billing errors, unauthorized charges, and financial exploitation. They can review facility contracts and billing statements, and work with facility administration to resolve payment disputes.

stateOverview.intro: Texas offers 12 major programs to help your aging loved one, 9 provide direct financial benefits or services, while 3 offer free guidance and advocacy. The financial help ranges from $298/month for groceries through SNAP to comprehensive healthcare coverage through STAR+PLUS that includes home care services. Free resources include one-on-one Medicare counseling, legal aid for seniors 60+, and nursing home advocacy. Most benefit programs have income limits around $2,000-3,000/month for a single person, but several valuable resources are available regardless of income. Start by checking if your loved one qualifies for Medicaid programs, these provide the most comprehensive coverage and can coordinate multiple types of care under one system.

startHere.name: STAR+PLUS
startHere.why: Combines full healthcare coverage with home care services if your loved one needs nursing home level care but wants to stay home.

startHere.name: SNAP
startHere.why: Quick way to reduce grocery costs by up to $298/month with relatively simple application process.

startHere.name: Texas SHIP
startHere.why: Free expert help navigating Medicare options and finding prescription drug savings, no income requirements.

byNeed.need: Comprehensive health coverage and home care
byNeed.description: Medicaid programs that provide full healthcare plus services to help your loved one stay at home instead of moving to a nursing facility.
Direct financial assistance for groceries, utility bills, and home energy efficiency improvements.
Programs that pay Medicare premiums, deductibles, and copays for qualifying low-income seniors.
Home-delivered meals and care coordination services for seniors who need daily assistance.
Expert help with Medicare decisions, legal issues, and nursing home problems, available regardless of income.

quickFacts: Texas uses YourTexasBenefits.com for SNAP and LIHEAP applications
PACE operates in limited Texas counties, availability depends on your loved one's location
Most Medicaid waiver programs in Texas have waiting lists, so apply early even if your loved one doesn't need services immediately
stateOverview.resourcesVsBenefits: Benefits like STAR+PLUS and SNAP require your loved one to meet specific income and asset limits, typically around $2,000-3,000/month income for a single person. Resources like Texas SHIP Medicare counseling, legal aid through Area Agencies on Aging, and the Long-Term Care Ombudsman are free to all Texas seniors regardless of income, making them good starting points while you determine benefit eligibility.
